entries.name: setup-runtime.bat
entries.description: Downloads and prepares the native runtime libraries used by compiled LSX projects.
entries.example: setup-runtime.bat
entries.exampleNote: Run this once before building projects that use GLFW, OpenGL, OpenAL, images, fonts, or other native bindings.
entries.friendlyDescription: Downloads and prepares the native runtime libraries used by compiled LSX projects.
entries.whatItIs: Downloads and prepares the native runtime libraries used by compiled LSX projects.
entries.whenToUse: Use on a new computer, after downloading a fresh toolkit, or when runtime DLLs are missing.
entries.beginnerNote: Run this once before building projects that use GLFW, OpenGL, OpenAL, images, fonts, or other native bindings.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: No cleanup is required unless the example creates an owned table or object.
entries.workflow: Double-click setup-runtime.bat from the toolkit root and wait for a successful finish.
entries.commonMistake: Do not run it from a copied project folder; run the script in the toolkit root.

entries.name: INSTALL_VSCODE_EXTENSION.bat
entries.description: Installs the bundled LazyScriptEX extension into Visual Studio Code.
entries.example: INSTALL_VSCODE_EXTENSION.bat
entries.exampleNote: Run it, restart VS Code, and open the complete toolkit root folder.
entries.friendlyDescription: Installs the bundled LazyScriptEX extension into Visual Studio Code.
entries.whatItIs: Installs the bundled LazyScriptEX extension into Visual Studio Code.
entries.whenToUse: Use before editing LSX so completion, hovers, syntax highlighting, and Problems-panel diagnostics are available.
entries.beginnerNote: Run it, restart VS Code, and open the complete toolkit root folder.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: No cleanup is required unless the example creates an owned table or object.
entries.workflow: Run it, restart VS Code, and open the complete toolkit root folder.

entries.name: build-all.bat
entries.description: Builds every bundled project example on the current computer.
entries.example: build-all.bat
entries.exampleNote: This optional step creates local example executables and is useful when Windows warns about downloaded prebuilt EXEs.
entries.friendlyDescription: Builds every bundled project example on the current computer.
entries.whatItIs: Builds every bundled project example on the current computer.
entries.whenToUse: Use after setup when you want fresh local builds of every example.
entries.beginnerNote: This optional step creates local example executables and is useful when Windows warns about downloaded prebuilt EXEs.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: No cleanup is required unless the example creates an owned table or object.
entries.workflow: This optional step creates local example executables and is useful when Windows warns about downloaded prebuilt EXEs.

entries.name: new-project.bat
entries.description: Copies the included ProjectTemplate into a new project folder and updates the output executable name.
entries.example: new-project.bat MyFirstGame
entries.exampleNote: Run this from Command Prompt in the toolkit root.
entries.friendlyDescription: Copies the included ProjectTemplate into a new project folder and updates the output executable name.
entries.whatItIs: Copies the included ProjectTemplate into a new project folder and updates the output executable name.
entries.whenToUse: Use whenever you want a correctly structured new LSX project.
entries.beginnerNote: Run this from Command Prompt in the toolkit root.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: No cleanup is required unless the example creates an owned table or object.
entries.workflow: Run this from Command Prompt in the toolkit root.

entries.name: main
entries.description: The entry function called when a compiled LSX executable starts.
entries.example:
fn main()
    console.write_line("Game started")
    return 0
end
entries.exampleNote: Return 0 for success or a non-zero code when startup fails.
entries.friendlyDescription: The entry function called when a compiled LSX executable starts.
entries.whatItIs: The entry function called when a compiled LSX executable starts.
entries.whenToUse: Every executable project needs an entry-capable main function.
entries.beginnerNote: Return 0 for success or a non-zero code when startup fails.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: No cleanup is required unless the example creates an owned table or object.
entries.workflow: Return 0 for success or a non-zero code when startup fails.

entries.name: local
entries.description: Creates a variable owned by the current function or block and lets the compiler infer its representation.
entries.example:
local speed = 6.5
local lives = 3
local title = "My Game"
local running = true
entries.exampleNote: Use a descriptive name and assign the first meaningful value.
entries.friendlyDescription: Creates a variable owned by the current function or block and lets the compiler infer its representation.
entries.whatItIs: Creates a variable owned by the current function or block and lets the compiler infer its representation.
entries.whenToUse: Use for changing game state, temporary calculations, handles, objects, and function-local resources.
entries.beginnerNote: Use a descriptive name and assign the first meaningful value.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: No cleanup is required unless the example creates an owned table or object.
entries.workflow: Use a descriptive name and assign the first meaningful value.

entries.name: const
entries.description: Creates a reusable module-level constant or object definition.
entries.exampleNote: Use .new() on an object definition when you need an owned runtime instance.
entries.friendlyDescription: Creates a reusable module-level constant or object definition.
entries.whatItIs: Creates a reusable module-level constant or object definition.
entries.whenToUse: Use for object templates, fixed configuration, and exported constants.
entries.beginnerNote: Use .new() on an object definition when you need an owned runtime instance.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: No cleanup is required unless the example creates an owned table or object.
entries.workflow: Use .new() on an object definition when you need an owned runtime instance.

entries.name: assignment
entries.description: Replaces the value stored by a variable or object field.
entries.example:
local health = 100
health = health - 25
entries.exampleNote: The value must remain compatible with the representation inferred for that variable.
entries.friendlyDescription: Replaces the value stored by a variable or object field.
entries.whatItIs: Replaces the value stored by a variable or object field.
entries.whenToUse: Use whenever gameplay changes state.
entries.beginnerNote: The value must remain compatible with the representation inferred for that variable.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: No cleanup is required unless the example creates an owned table or object.
entries.workflow: The value must remain compatible with the representation inferred for that variable.

entries.name: null
entries.description: Represents the absence of an object, handle, string, or other nullable value.
entries.example:
local selected_enemy = null
if selected_enemy == null then
    console.write_line("Nothing selected")
end
entries.exampleNote: Check for null before calling fields or methods on a value that may be missing.
entries.friendlyDescription: Represents the absence of an object, handle, string, or other nullable value.
entries.whatItIs: Represents the absence of an object, handle, string, or other nullable value.
entries.whenToUse: Use for optional selections, resources that have not loaded, and fields assigned later.
entries.beginnerNote: Check for null before calling fields or methods on a value that may be missing.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: No cleanup is required unless the example creates an owned table or object.
entries.workflow: Check for null before calling fields or methods on a value that may be missing.

entries.name: if
entries.description: Runs a block only when its condition is true.
entries.example:
if health <= 0 then
    console.write_line("Defeated")
end
entries.exampleNote: The block begins after then and ends at end.
entries.friendlyDescription: Runs a block only when its condition is true.
entries.whatItIs: Runs a block only when its condition is true.
entries.whenToUse: Use for gameplay rules, validation, input decisions, and error checks.
entries.beginnerNote: The block begins after then and ends at end.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: No cleanup is required unless the example creates an owned table or object.
entries.workflow: The block begins after then and ends at end.

entries.name: elseif and else
entries.signature: if condition then ... elseif other then ... else ... end
entries.description: Chooses exactly one branch from several possibilities.
entries.example:
if health <= 0 then
    console.write_line("Defeated")
elseif health < 30 then
    console.write_line("Hurt")
else
    console.write_line("Ready")
end
entries.exampleNote: Branches are tested from top to bottom.
entries.friendlyDescription: Chooses exactly one branch from several possibilities.
entries.whatItIs: Chooses exactly one branch from several possibilities.
entries.whenToUse: Use when a value belongs to one of several states.
entries.beginnerNote: Branches are tested from top to bottom.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: No cleanup is required unless the example creates an owned table or object.
entries.workflow: Branches are tested from top to bottom.

entries.name: while
entries.description: Repeats a block while its condition remains true.
entries.example:
local index = 0
local total = 0
while index < 5 do
    total = total + index
    index = index + 1
end
entries.exampleNote: Make sure something in the loop can eventually make the condition false.
entries.friendlyDescription: Repeats a block while its condition remains true.
entries.whatItIs: Repeats a block while its condition remains true.
entries.whenToUse: Use for game loops, retries, indexed table traversal, and repeated work.
entries.beginnerNote: Make sure something in the loop can eventually make the condition false.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: No cleanup is required unless the example creates an owned table or object.
entries.workflow: Make sure something in the loop can eventually make the condition false.

entries.name: for in
entries.signature: for item in table do ... end
entries.description: Reads every value from a growable inferred table in order.
entries.example:
local scores = {}
scores.push(10)
scores.push(20)
local total = 0
for score in scores do
    total = total + score
end
scores.destroy()
entries.exampleNote: The table must be a growable table inferred from {}, push, indexed writes, or another table-producing operation.
entries.friendlyDescription: Reads every value from a growable inferred table in order.
entries.whatItIs: Reads every value from a growable inferred table in order.
entries.whenToUse: Use when you need every item and do not need the index.
entries.beginnerNote: The table must be a growable table inferred from {}, push, indexed writes, or another table-producing operation.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: No cleanup is required unless the example creates an owned table or object.
entries.workflow: The table must be a growable table inferred from {}, push, indexed writes, or another table-producing operation.

entries.name: break
entries.description: Stops the nearest while or for loop immediately.
entries.example:
while true do
    if should_stop then break end
    update_game()
end
entries.exampleNote: Only use break inside a loop.
entries.friendlyDescription: Stops the nearest while or for loop immediately.
entries.whatItIs: Stops the nearest while or for loop immediately.
entries.whenToUse: Use when a result, error, or user action should end a loop early.
entries.beginnerNote: Only use break inside a loop.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: No cleanup is required unless the example creates an owned table or object.
entries.workflow: Only use break inside a loop.

entries.name: function declaration
entries.description: Defines reusable LSX behavior. Parameter and return representations are inferred from use.
entries.exampleNote: Call it with dot syntax when it is a method and normal name(args) syntax when it is a module function.
entries.friendlyDescription: Defines reusable LSX behavior. Parameter and return representations are inferred from use.
entries.whatItIs: Defines reusable LSX behavior. Parameter and return representations are inferred from use.
entries.whenToUse: Use to separate systems, remove repeated code, and name game behavior.
entries.beginnerNote: Call it with dot syntax when it is a method and normal name(args) syntax when it is a module function.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: No cleanup is required unless the example creates an owned table or object.
entries.workflow: Call it with dot syntax when it is a method and normal name(args) syntax when it is a module function.

entries.name: return
entries.description: Ends the current function and optionally gives a value back to the caller.
entries.exampleNote: All reachable return values in one function must infer compatible representations.
entries.friendlyDescription: Ends the current function and optionally gives a value back to the caller.
entries.whatItIs: Ends the current function and optionally gives a value back to the caller.
entries.whenToUse: Use for calculated results, status codes, created objects, and early exits.
entries.beginnerNote: All reachable return values in one function must infer compatible representations.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: No cleanup is required unless the example creates an owned table or object.
entries.workflow: All reachable return values in one function must infer compatible representations.

entries.name: early return
entries.signature: if failed then return code end
entries.description: Leaves a function as soon as a required condition fails.
entries.example:
if window == 0 then
    GLFW.glfwTerminate()
    return fail("Window creation failed.")
end
entries.exampleNote: Clean up anything already created before returning.
entries.friendlyDescription: Leaves a function as soon as a required condition fails.
entries.whatItIs: Leaves a function as soon as a required condition fails.
entries.whenToUse: Use to keep startup and validation code readable.
entries.beginnerNote: Clean up anything already created before returning.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: No cleanup is required unless the example creates an owned table or object.
entries.workflow: Clean up anything already created before returning.

entries.name: object table
entries.description: A named-field table that describes a structured LSX object.
entries.example:
const Player = {
    name = "Hero"
    health = 100
}
local player = Player.new()
player.health = 75
player.destroy()
entries.exampleNote: Named fields describe what the object stores; function fields become methods. Use const plus .new() for independent instances, or static const for one shared service.
entries.friendlyDescription: A named-field table that describes a structured LSX object.
entries.whatItIs: A named-field table that describes a structured LSX object.
entries.whenToUse: Use for players, components, resources, settings, events, and UI state.
entries.beginnerNote: Named fields describe what the object stores; function fields become methods. Use const plus .new() for independent instances, or static const for one shared service.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: Destroy owned instances created with .new() when no longer needed.
entries.workflow: Named fields describe what the object stores; function fields become methods.

entries.name: growable table
entries.description: An ordered table whose element layout is inferred from push, indexed assignment, a function result, or native context.
entries.example:
local enemies = {}
enemies.push(first_enemy)
enemies.push(second_enemy)
local count = enemies.length()
enemies.destroy()
entries.exampleNote: Use {} when the list must grow or shrink at runtime.
entries.friendlyDescription: An ordered table whose element layout is inferred from push, indexed assignment, a function result, or native context.
entries.whatItIs: An ordered table whose element layout is inferred from push, indexed assignment, a function result, or native context.
entries.whenToUse: Use for inventories, entities, UI rows, jobs, messages, and other changing lists.
entries.beginnerNote: Use {} when the list must grow or shrink at runtime.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: Call destroy() when the owned table is no longer needed.
entries.workflow: Use {} when the list must grow or shrink at runtime.

entries.name: fixed positional table
entries.description: A small fixed native value whose positions are stored contiguously.
entries.exampleNote: x/y/z/w and r/g/b/a alias the first four positions.
entries.friendlyDescription: A small fixed native value whose positions are stored contiguously.
entries.whatItIs: A small fixed native value whose positions are stored contiguously.
entries.whenToUse: Use for compact vectors, colors, dimensions, and small fixed groups of values.
entries.beginnerNote: x/y/z/w and r/g/b/a alias the first four positions.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: Destroy the owned positional value when finished.
entries.workflow: x/y/z/w and r/g/b/a alias the first four positions.

entries.name: flat numeric buffer
entries.description: A contiguous inferred numeric table passed directly to native APIs without per-element boxing.
entries.exampleNote: Decimal literals infer packed f32 storage; non-negative integer literals fitting 32 bits infer packed u32 storage.
entries.friendlyDescription: A contiguous inferred numeric table passed directly to native APIs without per-element boxing.
entries.whatItIs: A contiguous inferred numeric table passed directly to native APIs without per-element boxing.
entries.whenToUse: Use for vertices, indices, pixels, samples, and native data uploads.
entries.beginnerNote: Decimal literals infer packed f32 storage; non-negative integer literals fitting 32 bits infer packed u32 storage.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: Destroy the owned table after the native system no longer needs its CPU storage.
entries.workflow: Decimal literals infer packed f32 storage; non-negative integer literals fitting 32 bits infer packed u32 storage.

entries.name: push
entries.description: Adds a value to the end of a growable table.
entries.example:
local scores = {}
local index = scores.push(100)
local count = scores.length()
scores.destroy()
entries.exampleNote: The first pushed value can establish the table element layout.
entries.friendlyDescription: Runs the push operation provided by Language/Tables.
entries.whatItIs: Runs the push operation provided by Language/Tables. It operates on the current undefined instance.
entries.whenToUse: Use undefined.push() for the push operation shown in the example.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.

parameterDocs.value: The value to read, write, or apply.

entries.workflow: Choose the table form by the job instead of treating every brace literal as the same thing.

entries.name: get
entries.description: Reads a value from a growable table by zero-based index.
entries.example:
local scores = {}
scores.push(100)
local first_score = scores.get(0)
scores.destroy()
entries.exampleNote: Check length() before using an index that may be out of range.
entries.friendlyDescription: Reads a value from the API.
entries.whatItIs: Reads a value from the API. It operates on the current undefined instance.
entries.whenToUse: Use undefined.get() when you need the current value instead of changing state.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.workflow: Choose the table form by the job instead of treating every brace literal as the same thing.

entries.name: length
entries.description: Returns how many values are currently stored.
entries.example: local count = enemies.length()
entries.exampleNote: Valid indexes run from 0 through count - 1.
entries.friendlyDescription: Returns the number of items currently stored.
entries.whatItIs: Returns the number of items currently stored. It operates on the current undefined instance.
entries.whenToUse: Use undefined.length() for loops, empty checks, and bounds-aware indexing.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.workflow: Choose the table form by the job instead of treating every brace literal as the same thing.

entries.name: remove
entries.description: Removes one value and keeps the remaining order.
entries.example:
if inventory.remove(slot) then
    console.write_line("Item removed")
end
entries.exampleNote: Use remove_fast when order does not matter and maximum speed is preferred.
entries.friendlyDescription: Runs the remove operation provided by Language/Tables.
entries.whatItIs: Runs the remove operation provided by Language/Tables. It operates on the current undefined instance.
entries.whenToUse: Use undefined.remove() for the remove operation shown in the example.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.workflow: Choose the table form by the job instead of treating every brace literal as the same thing.

entries.name: remove_fast
entries.description: Removes one value by moving the final value into its slot, so ordering can change.
entries.example: enemies.remove_fast(defeated_index)
entries.exampleNote: Do not use this when indexes or visible order must stay stable.
entries.friendlyDescription: Runs the remove fast operation provided by Language/Tables.
entries.whatItIs: Runs the remove fast operation provided by Language/Tables. It operates on the current undefined instance.
entries.whenToUse: Use undefined.remove_fast() for the remove fast operation shown in the example.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.workflow: Choose the table form by the job instead of treating every brace literal as the same thing.

entries.name: clear
entries.description: Removes all values while retaining allocated capacity for later reuse.
entries.example: active_hits.clear()
entries.exampleNote: clear() does not destroy objects stored inside the table unless that API explicitly owns them.
entries.friendlyDescription: Removes the currently stored items or recorded work while keeping the object reusable.
entries.whatItIs: Removes the currently stored items or recorded work while keeping the object reusable. It operates on the current undefined instance.
entries.whenToUse: Use undefined.clear() before rebuilding the contents or starting a fresh frame/batch.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.workflow: Choose the table form by the job instead of treating every brace literal as the same thing.

entries.name: byte_length
entries.description: Returns the size of the table data in bytes.
entries.example: GL.glBufferData(GL.GL_ARRAY_BUFFER,vertices.byte_length(),vertices,GL.GL_STATIC_DRAW)
entries.exampleNote: Use it with native APIs that ask for a byte size rather than an element count.
entries.friendlyDescription: Runs the byte length operation provided by Language/Tables.
entries.whatItIs: Runs the byte length operation provided by Language/Tables. It operates on the current undefined instance.
entries.whenToUse: Use undefined.byte_length() for the byte length operation shown in the example.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.workflow: Choose the table form by the job instead of treating every brace literal as the same thing.

entries.name: new
entries.description: Creates an owned runtime object, applies field defaults, and calls its constructor when one is declared.
entries.example:
const Player = {
    name = ""
    health = 100

    constructor = fn(name, health)
        self.name = name
        self.health = health
    end
}

local player = Player.new("Luna", 150)
entries.exampleNote: Constructor arguments are inferred and passed after the object has been allocated and its defaults initialized.
entries.friendlyDescription: Creates an owned runtime object and runs its constructor with the supplied arguments.
entries.whatItIs: Creates an owned runtime object and runs its constructor with the supplied arguments.
entries.whenToUse: Use when each player, component, resource, controller, or behavior needs independent state. Do not use .new() for a static const object.
entries.beginnerNote: Constructor arguments are inferred and passed after field defaults are initialized.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: Call object.destroy() when the owned instance is no longer needed.

parameterDocs.arguments: Values accepted by the object constructor. Omit them when the object has no constructor or a zero-argument constructor.

entries.returnsDescription: A new owned instance of the object definition.
entries.workflow: Define fields, add constructor = fn(...) when setup is needed, create with Object.new(...), then destroy the owned instance.
entries.commonMistake: Do not call .new() on a static const object. Use object.clone() for an explicit copy; Object.new(existing) is only the legacy copy form for objects without constructors.

entries.name: constructor
entries.description: Defines initialization code that Object.new(...) runs after allocating the object and applying all field defaults.
entries.example:
const Transform = {
    position = null
    rotation = null
    scale = null

    constructor = fn(position, rotation, scale)
        self.position = position
        self.rotation = rotation
        self.scale = scale
    end
}

local transform = Transform.new({0, 0, 0}, {0, 0, 0}, {1, 1, 1})
entries.exampleNote: Constructors return nothing and are invoked through Object.new(...), not object.constructor(...).
entries.friendlyDescription: Initializes a newly allocated LSX object with inferred arguments.
entries.whatItIs: A special object method that initializes self when Object.new(...) creates an instance.
entries.whenToUse: Use when an object needs creation arguments, nested state setup, resource acquisition, or required initial relationships.
entries.beginnerNote: A parameterless base constructor runs automatically. When the base constructor needs arguments, put base.constructor(...) first in the derived constructor.
entries.memberSummary: Receives self automatically and accepts the arguments passed to Object.new(...).
entries.requires: No external library is required.
entries.cleanup: The constructor does not replace object.destroy(); release owned state during destruction or normal shutdown.
entries.returnsDescription: Constructors do not return a value.
entries.workflow: Declare constructor = fn(...), assign required fields through self, create the object with Object.new(...), and destroy it when ownership ends.
entries.commonMistake: Do not call object.constructor(...) manually. A parameterized base constructor must be called with base.constructor(...) as the first derived constructor statement.
entries.howToGet: Declare it as a function field named constructor inside an object table.

entries.name: dot field access
entries.description: Reads or changes a named field on an object.
entries.example:
player.health = player.health - 10
console.write_line(player.health)
entries.exampleNote: LSX front-end code uses dot access, not colon method syntax.
entries.friendlyDescription: Reads or changes a named field on an object.
entries.whatItIs: Reads or changes a named field on an object.
entries.whenToUse: Use for object state, props, settings, resources, and returned records.
entries.beginnerNote: LSX front-end code uses dot access, not colon method syntax.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: No cleanup is required unless the example creates an owned table or object.
entries.workflow: LSX front-end code uses dot access, not colon method syntax.

entries.name: dot method call
entries.description: Calls behavior stored on the object while the compiler supplies the current self object.
entries.example: player.take_damage(25)
entries.exampleNote: Use dot calls for all normal LSX methods.
entries.friendlyDescription: Calls behavior stored on the object while the compiler supplies the current self object.
entries.whatItIs: Calls behavior stored on the object while the compiler supplies the current self object.
entries.whenToUse: Use whenever an object exposes behavior such as update, destroy, refresh, load, or draw.
entries.beginnerNote: Use dot calls for all normal LSX methods.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: No cleanup is required unless the example creates an owned table or object.

parameterDocs.arguments: The arguments value required by this operation. See the practical example and module setup above for where it comes from.

entries.workflow: Use dot calls for all normal LSX methods.

entries.name: destroy
entries.description: Releases an owned object or table created by the current code.
entries.example: player.destroy()
entries.exampleNote: Only destroy values you own. Borrowed children, fields, and handles may be owned by another system; check the API cleanup note.
entries.friendlyDescription: Releases an owned object or table created by the current code.
entries.whatItIs: Releases an owned object or table created by the current code.
entries.whenToUse: Use during shutdown, resource replacement, scene unloading, and failed startup cleanup.
entries.beginnerNote: Only destroy values you own. Borrowed children, fields, and handles may be owned by another system; check the API cleanup note.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: No cleanup is required unless the example creates an owned table or object.
entries.workflow: Only destroy values you own. Borrowed children, fields, and handles may be owned by another system; check the API cleanup note.
entries.commonMistake: Do not call cleanup twice, and do not use the resource after cleanup.

entries.name: normal string
entries.description: Creates a normal UTF-8 string and processes escape sequences such as newline.
entries.example: local message = "Loading...\nPlease wait."
entries.exampleNote: Use double quotes for ordinary user-visible text and paths that need escapes.
entries.friendlyDescription: Creates a normal UTF-8 string and processes escape sequences such as newline.
entries.whatItIs: Creates a normal UTF-8 string and processes escape sequences such as newline.
entries.whenToUse: Use for names, UI labels, logs, file paths, shader errors, and messages.
entries.beginnerNote: Use double quotes for ordinary user-visible text and paths that need escapes.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: No cleanup is required unless the example creates an owned table or object.
entries.workflow: Use double quotes for ordinary user-visible text and paths that need escapes.

entries.name: raw string
entries.description: Creates multiline UTF-8 text without processing backslash escapes.
entries.example:
local shader = `#version 460 core
void main() { }`
entries.exampleNote: Backticks are useful for shaders, JSON, and other embedded text containing quotes.
entries.friendlyDescription: Creates multiline UTF-8 text without processing backslash escapes.
entries.whatItIs: Creates multiline UTF-8 text without processing backslash escapes.
entries.whenToUse: Use when preserving the source text exactly is clearer than escaped strings.
entries.beginnerNote: Backticks are useful for shaders, JSON, and other embedded text containing quotes.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: No cleanup is required unless the example creates an owned table or object.
entries.workflow: Backticks are useful for shaders, JSON, and other embedded text containing quotes.

entries.name: null check
entries.description: Tests whether an optional object or reference is absent.
entries.example:
if selected == null then
    return 0
end
selected.update()
entries.exampleNote: Check before field or method access when an API can return null.
entries.friendlyDescription: Tests whether an optional object or reference is absent.
entries.whatItIs: Tests whether an optional object or reference is absent.
entries.whenToUse: Use for failed searches, optional selections, and not-yet-created resources.
entries.beginnerNote: Check before field or method access when an API can return null.
entries.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
entries.cleanup: No cleanup is required unless the example creates an owned table or object.
entries.workflow: Check before field or method access when an API can return null.

Language/Control flow.title: Conditions and loops
Language/Control flow.whatItIs: The statements that choose branches and repeat work.
Language/Control flow.whenToUse: Use for game rules, update loops, validation, and table traversal.
Language/Control flow.beginnerStart: Learn if/elseif/else, while, for-in, and break.
Language/Control flow.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
Language/Control flow.cleanup: Follow the ownership note shown by the example and API entry.

Language/Functions.title: Functions and returns
Language/Functions.whatItIs: Reusable named behavior with inferred parameters and results.
Language/Functions.whenToUse: Use to organize every game and engine system.
Language/Functions.beginnerStart: Write fn name(args), return a value when needed, and close with end.
Language/Functions.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
Language/Functions.cleanup: Follow the ownership note shown by the example and API entry.

Language/Getting started.title: Install and start LazyScriptEX
Language/Getting started.whatItIs: The required startup scripts and project creation workflow.
Language/Getting started.whenToUse: Use this before writing or running a project.
Language/Getting started.beginnerStart: Run setup-runtime.bat, install the VS Code extension, then create a project from the template.
Language/Getting started.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
Language/Getting started.cleanup: Follow the ownership note shown by the example and API entry.

Language/Objects.title: Objects and ownership
Language/Objects.whatItIs: Independent runtime instances with fields, methods, constructors, inheritance, and explicit ownership. For one shared manager instead, use a static object.
Language/Objects.whenToUse: Use for players, components, resources, windows, documents, and engine systems.
Language/Objects.beginnerStart: Create instances with .new(...), use constructor = fn(...) for required setup, use dot access, and destroy what you own. Use static const when no instance should be created.
Language/Objects.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
Language/Objects.cleanup: Follow the ownership note shown by the example and API entry.

Language/Strings and null.title: Strings and optional values
Language/Strings and null.whatItIs: UTF-8 normal/raw strings and the null value used for absence.
Language/Strings and null.whenToUse: Use for text, paths, shaders, JSON, messages, and optional references.
Language/Strings and null.beginnerStart: Use double quotes for escaped text, backticks for raw text, and check nullable values before access.
Language/Strings and null.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
Language/Strings and null.cleanup: Follow the ownership note shown by the example and API entry.

Language/Tables.title: Tables, objects, lists, and buffers
Language/Tables.whatItIs: The brace-based data forms used throughout LSX.
Language/Tables.whenToUse: Use named object tables for structured state, growable tables for changing lists, and positional numeric tables for native buffers.
Language/Tables.beginnerStart: Choose the table form by the job instead of treating every brace literal as the same thing.
Language/Tables.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
Language/Tables.cleanup: Follow the ownership note shown by the example and API entry.

Language/Values and variables.title: Values and variables
Language/Values and variables.whatItIs: The everyday inferred values stored by local variables and constants.
Language/Values and variables.whenToUse: Use in every function and object.
Language/Values and variables.beginnerStart: Start with local name = value and let the compiler infer the representation.
Language/Values and variables.requires: No external library is required unless the example imports one.
Language/Values and variables.cleanup: Follow the ownership note shown by the example and API entry.
